entry.title: Using Istio ServiceEntry for Bare-Metal Cockpit Access
entry.content:
Using Istio ServiceEntry for Bare-Metal Cockpit Access

SAP Community, 2026-07-13

Three Linux boxes. Two Ubuntu, one SUSE. Different install paths to get Cockpit running. Same port. Same Istio config. Same access URL from anywhere in the mesh.

That's the story.

This isn't really a post about Cockpit — Cockpit just happened to be the messiest service we needed to expose. Substitute your legacy admin UI, monitoring dashboard, or vendor console; the pattern doesn't change.

The setup

We run a fleet of self-managed Kubernetes clusters — k3d on bare-metal boxes.

  • Each cluster has an edge toolbox: a set of services that let us reach the underlying host from inside the mesh.
  • One of those services is Cockpit — the browser-based Linux system console running on port 9090.

The three boxes have been provisioned in the SAP Cloud Infrastructure (SCI):

 

HostOSHow Cockpit runs
192.0.2.67 (k3d-suse)SLES 15-SP5Docker container — quay.io/cockpit/ws --network=host (no native package available)
192.0.2.143 (k3d-ubuntu)Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTSNative systemd service — with an nsswitch DynamicUser fix
192.0.2.20 (k3d-ubuntu-sap)Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTSNative systemd service — with an nsswitch DynamicUser fix

The install stories are completely different. The Istio config is identical. That's the point.

The ServiceEntry pattern

An Istio ServiceEntry registers an external host — something running outside the mesh, outside the cluster — as a named, routable service inside the mesh.

From that point on, anything running in the mesh can reach it by name, and Istio applies all the usual controls: mTLS, traffic policies, access rules, metrics.

For Cockpit, three resources do the work:

1. ServiceEntry — register the external host

apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: ServiceEntry
metadata:
  name: cockpit-tls
  namespace: cockpit
spec:
  hosts:
    - cockpit.tls
  addresses:
    - 192.0.2.67/32       # e.g. k3d-suse
  location: MESH_EXTERNAL
  ports:
    - name: https
      number: 9090
      protocol: TLS
  resolution: STATIC
  endpoints:
    - address: 192.0.2.67
 

The addresses field is what makes this a proper static entry — Istio knows exactly which IP to route to, no DNS resolution required.

2. DestinationRule — handle Cockpit's self-signed TLS

apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: DestinationRule
metadata:
  name: cockpit
  namespace: cockpit
spec:
  host: cockpit.tls
  trafficPolicy:
    tls:
      mode: SIMPLE
      insecureSkipVerify: true   # Cockpit uses a self-signed cert
 
  • ✅ SIMPLE mode → Istio originates TLS to the external host.
  • 👉 insecureSkipVerify: true → Cockpit's self-signed cert isn't in any CA bundle, and that's fine here.

Why it's fine: the trust boundary is the mesh itself, not the leaf certificate. The mesh authenticates both endpoints; the leaf cert just carries the TLS layer, not identity.

3. VirtualService — expose via the ingress gateway

apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: cockpit-ext
  namespace: cockpit
spec:
  gateways:
    - <mesh-gateway-ns>/<ingress-gateway>
  hosts:
    - cockpit.<cluster>.example.com
    - cockpit.btp-<cluster>.example.com
  http:
    - match:
        - method:
            regex: ^(GET|POST|PUT|DELETE|PATCH|OPTIONS)$
          uri:
            prefix: /
      route:
        - destination:
            host: cockpit.tls
            port:
              number: 9090
      timeout: 300s
 

Two TLS legs, one clean result:

  • ✅The gateway terminates external TLS (real cert, signed by our CA).
  • ✅The VirtualService re-originates TLS to the Cockpit backend via the ServiceEntry.
  • 👉The self-signed cert on the backend never reaches a browser.

What this looks like end-to-end

Browser (HTTPS, real cert)
    │
    ▼
Istio Ingress Gateway
    │ TLS termination
    │ VirtualService: cockpit.<cluster>.example.com → cockpit.tls:9090
    ▼
ServiceEntry: cockpit.tls
    │ DestinationRule: TLS SIMPLE, insecureSkipVerify
    │ MESH_EXTERNAL, STATIC, 192.0.2.67/32
    ▼
Cockpit on bare metal (port 9090, self-signed cert)
    │
    ├── Ubuntu: systemd cockpit.socket (native install)
    └── SUSE:   docker container quay.io/cockpit/ws --network=host
 

The browser sees a valid cert. Cockpit sees an HTTPS request. What's running underneath — a systemd socket or a Docker container, Ubuntu packaging or SUSE workarounds — is invisible to the mesh.

The OS differences are noise

To make the point concrete: here's what it took to get Cockpit running on each box.

Ubuntu:

sudo apt install cockpit
sudo systemctl enable --now cockpit.socket
# Plus: fix /etc/nsswitch.conf to add 'systemd' to passwd/group lines
# (DynamicUser bug — see appendix)
 

SUSE 15-SP5:

# No native package available — blocked by Python 3.13 dependency
docker run -d --name cockpit-ws \
  --privileged --pid=host --ipc=host \
  -v /:/host:rw,rslave \
  --network=host \
  --restart=unless-stopped \
  quay.io/cockpit/ws
 

Different commands. Different package managers. One uses systemd, one uses Docker.

👉None of that appears in the Istio config. The ServiceEntry doesn't know or care whether a systemd socket or a Docker container is behind 192.0.2.67:9090 — it just knows the IP and the port.

That's the architectural point:

  • ✅ Istio ServiceEntry decouples "how the service runs" from "how the mesh reaches it."
  • ✅The mesh sees a named endpoint.
  • ✅The infrastructure below can be anything.

What you get from the mesh

Once the ServiceEntry is in place, everything the mesh provides applies to the external service automatically:

  • Consistent access URL — https://cockpit.<cluster>.example.com regardless of the host's IP or OS
  • TLS everywhere — real cert externally, Istio-managed TLS to the backend
  • Mesh observability — request counts, latency, error rates in Kiali and SAP Cloud Logging
  • Access policy — Istio AuthorizationPolicy can gate access at the mesh layer before traffic reaches Cockpit's own login
  • No VPN, no port-forwarding — the mesh handles routing

The last point matters for fleet operations: as the number of bare-metal hosts grows, the access pattern stays the same. One URL format, one TLS story, one place to apply policy.

Authentication: SSO in front, PAM behind

The mesh gives us a clean place to layer authentication without touching Cockpit itself. We shipped it — and the road there taught us exactly what Cockpit will and won't accept. Here's the working design and the trap we fell into.

First, the perimeter. Security here is defense-in-depth, and the outermost layer isn't the mesh — it's the corporate network. These Cockpit boxes are SAP Linux hosts behind the corporate firewall; cockpit.<cluster> is reachable only from the corporate network or via VPN into it, never from the public internet. Everything below — SSO at the mesh, PAM on the host — is additional gating layered on top of that closed perimeter, not the only thing between the box and the world.

  • 👉 Layer 0 — network: corporate firewall / VPN. No public exposure, full stop.
  • ✅ Layer 1 — who may reach it: Keycloak / Entra ID SSO at the mesh.
  • ✅ Layer 2 — what they can do: Cockpit PAM + sudo on the host.

What we built

An oauth2-proxy pod in the cockpit namespace, backed by Keycloak (realm <realm>, federated to Entra ID), using an existing PKCE public client (<client-id>). It's fronted by the same hostname the service already used — cockpit.<cluster>.example.com — with a single feature flag (cockpit_auth_create) that flips the VirtualService backend:

  • off → the route points straight at the ServiceEntry (cockpit.tls) — Cockpit's own PAM login, no SSO.
  • on → the route points at the oauth2-proxy pod, which authenticates against Keycloak and then forwards to the same bare-metal Cockpit.

One host, one toggle — no separate cockpit-oauth2. hostname, and deliberately no unauthenticated break-glass path: when auth is on, SSO is the only way in. (Break-glass, if ever needed, is flipping the flag off and re-applying — an auditable config change, not a hidden back door.)

                    Browser (HTTPS, real cert)
                             │
                             ▼
                    Istio Ingress Gateway
                    (TLS termination, real cert)
                             │
                    VirtualService: cockpit.<cluster>
                    route backend chosen by cockpit_auth_create
                             │
              auth OFF ──────┴────── auth ON
                 │                      │
                 │                      ▼
                 │               oauth2-proxy pod
                 │               (PKCE, session cookie)
                 │                      │
                 │                      ▼─────► Keycloak <realm>
                 │                               (federated to Entra ID)
                 │                      │
                 └──────────┬───────────┘
                            ▼
                    ServiceEntry: cockpit.tls
                    (STATIC, 192.0.2.67/32)
                            │
                            ▼
                    Cockpit on bare metal :9090
                    (self-signed cert · PAM auth)
 

Two independent gates protect the box — on top of the network perimeter (firewall/VPN) described above:

  • Gate 1 — who may reach it: Keycloak / Entra ID SSO, enforced by oauth2-proxy.
  • Gate 2 — what they can do: Cockpit PAM + sudo, enforced by the host's own login.

Both gates matter because Cockpit is PAM-only — it can't consume the corporate identity itself (more on that below), so the mesh gate and the host gate stay independent.

The proxy's essential args:

- --provider=keycloak-oidc
- --client-id=<client-id>
- --client-secret=unused                 # required flag even for PUBLIC PKCE clients
- --oidc-issuer-url=https://keycloak.<cluster>.example.com/realms/<realm>
- --upstream=https://192.0.2.67:9090     # ServiceEntry hostname isn't DNS-resolvable from the pod
- --ssl-upstream-insecure-skip-verify=true
- --code-challenge-method=S256           # PKCE — no client secret needed
- --oidc-extra-audience=account          # Keycloak tokens carry aud: [account]
- --redirect-url=https://cockpit.<cluster>.example.com/oauth2/callback
 

The trap: never forward a Bearer token to Cockpit

The proxy worked — Entra ID login succeeded, the session cookie was set. And then Cockpit rendered its own login screen anyway. A double login. The user was already authenticated by the corporate IdP, yet Cockpit demanded credentials again.

The culprit was one innocent-looking flag we'd added: --pass-authorization-header=true. It forwards the Keycloak OIDC access token to the upstream as:

Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6…
 

Here's the thing: Cockpit's cockpit-ws is PAM-only. On the Authorization header it understands exactly two things:

  1. Basic base64(user:password) → handed straight to PAM (/etc/pam.d/cockpit → SSSD/local), or
  2. no header → serve the interactive login form.

It has no OIDC/JWT verifier, and there is no cockpit.conf option to trust a forwarded identity header — no X-Auth-Request-User trust mode, nothing.

So when Cockpit received Authorization: Bearer <jwt>:

  • 👉It wasn't Basic → Cockpit couldn't extract a username or password.
  • 👉PAM had nothing to chew on.
  • 👉Cockpit did the only thing left: it showed its login form.

The token we sent to prove the user had authenticated was the very thing breaking the flow.

The solution is a single commented-out line:

# - --pass-authorization-header=true
 

With no Authorization header, oauth2-proxy simply proxies the already-authenticated request, and Cockpit behaves as it does for any fresh connection: it presents its PAM login. The user signs in as a real local Linux account (<local-user>, first.last, …).

This isn't a workaround — it's a better model

Losing the "single sign-on all the way through" reflex turns out to be a feature. You end up with two independent gates:

Gate 1 — WHO may reach the box     : Keycloak / Entra ID SSO   (oauth2-proxy)
Gate 2 — WHAT they can do on it    : Cockpit PAM — local user + sudo
 
  • ✅The corporate IdP decides who is even allowed to see Cockpit.
  • ✅The host's own PAM/sudo model decides their privileges.

And what you don't carry:

  • ✅No shared service account to leak.
  • ✅No per-user local password to juggle.
  • ✅No sidecar to maintain.
  • ✅Cockpit's audit log records the real Linux user, not a generic proxy identity.

Alternatives we rejected

ApproachWhy it doesn't work?
--pass-basic-auth=true + shared cockpit-sso accountoauth2-proxy sends the logged-in user's email as the Basic username (only the password is static). You can't force a fixed shared username, so PAM never matches.
Uniform local password per user (passwd 'user@sap.com')Needs a local account named per email plus a shared, non-secret password. Doesn't scale.
nginx sidecar injecting fixed Basic credsWorks, but adds a container + ConfigMap for no gain over the two-gate model.
Cockpit trusting X-Auth-Request-UserUnsupported — no such config exists in Cockpit.
Mesh-layer JWT (RequestAuthentication + AuthorizationPolicy)See below — our ingress gateway isn't in the ambient mesh, so this never intercepts the traffic.

Why not enforce JWT at the mesh layer?

The tempting Istio-native answer is RequestAuthentication + AuthorizationPolicy on the ingress gateway — validate the JWT before traffic ever reaches the backend. We tried it.

👉It doesn't work in this topology:

  • The ingress gateway routes directly to the ServiceEntry/host.
  • It isn't part of the ambient mesh, so a waypoint proxy never receives the traffic.
  • The policy is never enforced (AncestorNotBound).

✅An in-cluster oauth2-proxy pod sidesteps the whole problem:

  • Works with a plain sidecar-mode gateway.
  • Handles the OAuth2 code flow and session cookies for browser sessions.
  • Needs zero changes to Cockpit.

👉Takeaway: 

  • Cockpit is PAM-only.
  • Never forward an OIDC/JWT bearer token to it — send Basic credentials it can validate, or send nothing and let its PAM login handle authentication behind the SSO gate.

The pattern generalises

Nothing here is Cockpit-specific. The whole approach — a ServiceEntry fronting a service that runs directly on the host machine, exposed through the mesh — works for anything on that box: a database, a monitoring UI, a metrics endpoint, a vendor admin console. Cockpit was just the messiest example.

The same three resources would bridge an in-host PostgreSQL, HANA, or MongoDB into the mesh just as well — point the ServiceEntry at the host IP and the DB port, and every consumer inside the mesh reaches it by a stable name with Istio-managed TLS, regardless of which box it physically runs on. Swap the host IP and port, keep the pattern.

The SSO front door generalises the same way: one hostname, an *_auth_create toggle flipping the VirtualService backend between direct and SSO-gated. Put it in front of whatever the host is running.

What differs per backend is how far the identity travels, and Cockpit sits at one clear end of that spectrum:

  • 👉Cockpit is PAM-only — it can't consume a forwarded corporate identity at all.
  • ✅So we stop the token at the proxy and let two independent gates stand: SSO decides who reaches the box, PAM decides what they can do.
  • 👉The price is two logins, by necessity — and that's the right trade for a backend that speaks only PAM.

The rule that falls out of this, and the one thing to carry away:

  • ✅ PAM-style backends want the token withheld — forward it and you get the double-login trap from earlier.
  • 👉Before you set any --pass-* flag, know what your backend does with an identity header. A backend that can consume forwarded identity plays by opposite rules — but that's a different service, and a different post.

Fleet management

All three boxes are part of the Kyma Unplugged fleet. The Istio resources — ServiceEntry, DestinationRule, VirtualService, namespace, RBAC — are rendered per-cluster from a single template with the host IP as a variable.

  • ✅Adding a new bare-metal host means adding one entry to a variables file.
  • ✅The Istio config template doesn't change.

(See the companion post on autonomous diagnostics for how KRO manages the wider fleet.)

The pattern generalises — the result is the same regardless of what's below:

  • ✅A consistent access URL.
  • ✅TLS managed at the mesh layer.
  • ✅One place to apply policy.

👉The infrastructure differences below the socket boundary stop mattering.

Resources

 

WhatWhere
Cockpit install + Linux admin referenceSee Appendix below
Istio ServiceEntry docshttps://istio.io/latest/docs/reference/config/networking/service-entry/
oauth2-proxyhttps://oauth2-proxy.github.io/oauth2-proxy/
Companion post: autonomous diagnostics on Kyma Unpluggedhttps://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/autonomous-diagnostics-on-kyma-unplugged-goose-litellm-kro-sap-ai-core-in/ba-p/14435287

Glossary

 

TermMeaning
PAMPluggable Authentication Modules — the Linux/Unix authentication framework. Cockpit delegates login to PAM (/etc/pam.d/cockpit), which is why it accepts a local user + password (or Basic auth) but not an OIDC/JWT token.
ServiceEntryIstio resource that registers an external host (outside the mesh/cluster) as a named, routable service inside the mesh.
DestinationRuleIstio resource defining how traffic to a host is handled — here, originating TLS to Cockpit's self-signed cert.
VirtualServiceIstio resource that routes matching requests (by host/path) to a destination — the ingress-to-backend wiring.
MESH_EXTERNALServiceEntry location marking the target as living outside the mesh (a bare-metal host), not an in-cluster workload.
SSOSingle Sign-On — one corporate login (here Keycloak, federated to Entra ID) granting access across services.
OIDC / PKCEOpenID Connect — the SSO protocol; Proof Key for Code Exchange — the OAuth2 flow used by public clients (no client secret).
oauth2-proxyThe reverse proxy that performs the OIDC/Keycloak login and gates access before traffic reaches the backend.
NSSName Service Switch (/etc/nsswitch.conf) — Linux config for how user/group lookups resolve; the systemd entry is required for Cockpit's DynamicUser.

Stack: Istio · Kyma Unplugged · oauth2-proxy · Keycloak · Cockpit · Ubuntu · SLES 15-SP5

Appendix — Linux Admin: User Management & Cockpit Install

Reference for the Ubuntu and SUSE boxes behind the mesh. This is the host-side groundwork the ServiceEntry pattern above sits on top of.

Adding an admin user

Ubuntu / Debian

sudo adduser <username>          # creates home dir, prompts for password
sudo usermod -aG sudo <username> # grant sudo
 

Or for an existing user:

sudo passwd <username>           # set/change password
sudo usermod -aG sudo <username>
 

Verify:

groups <username>   # should include 'sudo'
 

SUSE / SLES

adduser does not exist on SUSE. Use useradd:

sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash <username>   # -m = create home, -s = shell
sudo passwd <username>                     # set password
sudo usermod -aG wheel <username>          # wheel = sudo equivalent on SUSE
 

Verify:

groups <username>   # should include 'wheel'
 

Distro difference: Ubuntu uses the sudo group; SUSE uses the wheel group. Members of wheel can run sudo commands.

Quick reference

 

TaskUbuntuSUSE
Create useradduser <user>useradd -m -s /bin/bash <user>
Set passwordprompted by adduserpasswd <user>
Grant adminusermod -aG sudo <user>usermod -aG wheel <user>
Verify groupsgroups <user>groups <user>
Admin groupsudowheel

Cockpit web console — installation & setup

Cockpit provides a browser-based system admin UI at port 9090.

Ubuntu (native install)

sudo apt install cockpit
sudo systemctl enable --now cockpit.socket
 

If it starts but returns empty responses, see the nsswitch solution below.

SUSE / SLES 15-SP5 (Docker container — native install not available)

Cockpit is not in any accessible repo for SLES 15-SP5. Use the official Docker image.

# Remove any previous failed attempt
docker rm cockpit-ws 2>/dev/null

# Start the container
docker run -d --name cockpit-ws \
  --privileged \
  --pid=host \
  --ipc=host \
  -v /:/host:rw,rslave \
  --network=host \
  --restart=unless-stopped \
  quay.io/cockpit/ws

# Verify
docker ps --filter name=cockpit-ws
curl -k -I https://localhost:9090/
 

Access: https://<host-ip>:9090/ — log in with system credentials.

Why --network=host and not -p 9090:9090? The container's startup script binds the listener before Docker's port mapping is active, causing [Errno 98] Address already in use. --network=host shares the host network stack directly and avoids this.

Why --privileged --pid=host -v /:/host? Cockpit needs to inspect and manage the host system — processes, services, storage, networking. These flags give it the required access.

--restart=unless-stopped ensures the container comes back after reboots or Docker daemon restarts.

Cockpit troubleshooting — Ubuntu DynamicUser / NSS solution

Symptom

curl -k -I https://localhost:9090/ returns an empty reply despite the socket being active. journalctl shows:

Failed to update dynamic user credentials: User or group with specified name already exists.
status=217/USER
 

Root cause

/etc/nsswitch.conf is missing systemd from the passwd: and group: lines.

Quick diagnosis

grep -E "^(passwd|group):" /etc/nsswitch.conf && systemctl --failed
 

If systemd is absent from those lines and you see failed cockpit-*-user.service units → this is the cause.

Solution

sudo cp /etc/nsswitch.conf /etc/nsswitch.conf.bak

# Append systemd (adjust pattern to match your actual line)
sudo sed -i -E 's/^(passwd:\s+files sss)$/\1 systemd/' /etc/nsswitch.conf
sudo sed -i -E 's/^(group:\s+files sss)$/\1 systemd/' /etc/nsswitch.conf

# Verify
grep -E "^(passwd|group):" /etc/nsswitch.conf
# Expected: passwd: files sss systemd / group: files sss systemd

# Restart
sudo systemctl reset-failed
sudo systemctl enable --now cockpit.socket
curl -k -I https://localhost:9090/   # should return HTTP/1.1 200 OK
 

SUSE note: SUSE usually already has systemd in nsswitch. If the container-based Cockpit has issues on SUSE, check docker logs cockpit-ws first — the cause will be different.

Alternative: Cockpit as a Kubernetes workload (in-cluster)

The ServiceEntry approach above bridges the mesh to an external bare-metal service. There is a cleaner alternative when you're starting fresh: run Cockpit as a proper Kubernetes workload inside the cluster, using hostPath volumes to access the underlying node.

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cockpit-ws
  namespace: cockpit
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cockpit-ws
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: cockpit-ws
    spec:
      hostPID: true
      hostIPC: true
      hostNetwork: false          # mesh handles networking
      containers:
        - name: cockpit-ws
          image: quay.io/cockpit/ws
          securityContext:
            privileged: true
          ports:
            - containerPort: 9090
          volumeMounts:
            - name: host-root
              mountPath: /host
      volumes:
        - name: host-root
          hostPath:
            path: /
            type: Directory
 

With Cockpit running as a pod, a standard Kubernetes Service + Istio VirtualService replaces the ServiceEntry entirely — no MESH_EXTERNAL, no DestinationRule with insecureSkipVerify. The pod is mesh-native from day one.

Trade-offs vs the ServiceEntry approach:

 

ConcernServiceEntryIn-cluster pod
Mesh integrationVia ServiceEntry + DestinationRuleNative — no special resources
Pod securityN/Aprivileged: true, hostPID, hostIPC
Node accessDirect (runs on the node)Via hostPath mount
OS independence✅same config regardless of OS✅same — container abstracts OS
Scales to multi-nodeOne ServiceEntry per hostOne pod per node (DaemonSet)
  • ✅ In-cluster pod — cleaner from a mesh perspective (mesh-native, no ServiceEntry/DestinationRule).
  • ✅ ServiceEntry — simpler operationally when Cockpit already runs on the host for other reasons (direct SSH access, local monitoring).

👉Both are valid — it depends on whether the host already runs Cockpit independently.

entry.title: Re: Custom Form with Custom Data Source in SAP Public Cloud (Grow with SAP)
entry.content:
Hello @Gourab_Dey ,

How can the translation be implemented in such cases?

In the SAP standard system, there is a template (XDP) for each language, and the relevant translations (e.g. for captions) are loaded at runtime.

Should the translations be incorporated into the form along with the data (i.e. captions via a binding)?

Or should there be two templates, as in the standard system, e.g. ZMM_PR_OUTPUT_DE and ZMM_PR_OUTPUT_EN, with a selection made in the coding?

Which approach is best practice?

Kind regards

entry.title: Adaptive Job Server Watch Not Sending Email Alerts
entry.content:
Hi Team,

I have a question regarding the BO servers.

We have configured watches for all BO servers in the CMC Monitoring tab to monitor their running state. The expectation is that whenever any server changes to the Stopped state, an email alert should be triggered to our mailbox so that we can take timely action.

Recently, we also created a watch for the Adaptive Job Server. However, we are not receiving any email alerts when this server stops.

To verify the configuration, we stopped the other BO servers and confirmed that email alerts are triggered successfully in the Development, QA, Test, and Production environments. The issue is occurring only with the Adaptive Job Server across all environments.

Our BO version details are as follows:

  • Product: SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform

  • Version: 4.2 Support Pack 9 Patch 13

  • Build: 14.2.9.4527

Could you please help us understand:

  1. Why are email alerts not being triggered for the Adaptive Job Server?

  2. Is monitoring the Adaptive Job Server using a watch expected to generate alerts when it stops, or is this server excluded from such monitoring by design?

Your assistance would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Best practice for multiple Reporting Entities in Statutory Report
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am working on an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition rollout and I would like to clarify the recommended approach for configuring Reporting Entities for statutory reporting.

The customer has two different legal entities (two different Company Codes), each with its own Tax Identification Number and Tax Office.

Example:

Company Code A
Portuguese legal entity with its own NIF
Tax Office: [Tax Office A]
Company Code B
Different Portuguese legal entity with a different NIF
Tax Office: [Tax Office B]

In the Statutory Reporting configuration, under:

Reporting Entity → Enter Parameters Specific to Reporting Entity

there are parameters such as PT_TA_CAE.

My questions are:

For two different legal entities with different NIFs, is the recommended approach to create two separate Reporting Entities (one per legal entity), or can both Company Codes be assigned to the same Reporting Entity with one Leading Organizational Unit?
If multiple Company Codes are assigned to the same Reporting Entity, how are entity-specific parameters such as PT_TA_OFFICE and Tax Identification Number handled?
Is there any SAP Best Practice or recommendation for localization when multiple legal entities are implemented in the same S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system?

Any guidance or experience from similar implementations would be highly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: SAP Business AI Platform: What It Means for SAP BTP & SAP BDC Customers – Live Expert Session
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced one of its most significant platform announcements in recent years: the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP). By bringing together SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), and SAP Business Transformation Management (BTM), SAP has outlined a vision for how enterprises can build, govern, and scale AI-driven business processes across their landscapes.

While the announcement generated substantial interest, it has also raised an important question within the SAP community:

What does this actually mean for existing SAP BTP and SAP Business Data Cloud customers?

 

For many architects, developers, platform owners, data professionals, and SAP user group members, the strategic direction is exciting—but the practical implications are less clear.

  • Is BAIP primarily a rebranding exercise or a significant evolution of SAP's platform strategy?
  • How will BTP fit into the new landscape?
  • What role does Business Data Cloud play in enabling AI?
  • Will customers need to change their current investments, architectures, or roadmaps?
  • What does this mean for day-to-day operations and technical teams?

These are exactly the questions the SAP community is asking—and exactly the questions we aim to answer.

Bringing Clarity from the Experts

To help SAP customers navigate this important announcement, I have organized a special live community session featuring leading SAP experts who are directly involved in shaping the future of these technologies.

Live Session

SAP Business AI Platform: What It Means for SAP BTP & SAP BDC Customers

📅 Date: July 29, 2026
🕓 Time: 16:00

What We Will Cover

During this session, we will revisit the strategic vision behind the SAP Business AI Platform and discuss how SAP intends to unify platform, data, and transformation capabilities to make agentic AI a practical reality for customers and partners.

The discussion will focus specifically on what BAIP means for:

  • SAP BTP customers
  • SAP Business Data Cloud customers
  • Enterprise architects
  • Application developers
  • Data and analytics professionals
  • Platform operations teams
  • SAP transformation leaders

Our experts will examine both the strategic and operational dimensions of the announcement:

Strategic Perspective

  • The vision behind SAP Business AI Platform
  • How BAIP reshapes the SAP platform landscape
  • The relationship between BTP, BDC, and BTM
  • Implications for customer investment strategies
  • The role of business AI in SAP's future roadmap

Practitioner Perspective

  • Impacts on platform architecture and governance
  • What developers and architects need to know
  • How data foundations evolve with Business Data Cloud
  • Practical considerations for current SAP BTP and SAP BDC users

Meet the Experts

We are fortunate to welcome an exceptional panel of SAP thought leaders:

  • Steffen Schad – Head of Outbound Product Management, SAP BTP
  • KP Sauer – Senior Director Data Warehousing, SAP
  • Dan Kearnan – Senior Director, SAP BTP

This is a unique opportunity to hear directly from experts who can provide both strategic context and practical guidance on how SAP's latest platform direction affects customers today and in the future.

Join the Conversation

SAP user groups have always played a critical role in helping customers understand major technology transitions. With the introduction of the SAP Business AI Platform, many organizations are evaluating how their existing BTP and Business Data Cloud investments fit into the bigger picture.

My goal with this session is simple: to bring clarity to the community and help SAP professionals understand what SAP BAIP means beyond the headlines.

Whether you are responsible for platform strategy, architecture, development, analytics, or data management, this discussion will provide valuable insights into SAP's evolving AI-first platform vision.

Register Now

🔗 Registration:
https://events.sap.com/baip/en_us/home.html

Watch Live on YouTube

📺 Live Stream:
https://youtube.com/live/6r2MNgbrT4Q

Add to Your Calendar

📅 Calendar Link:
https://community.sap.com/khhcw49343/downloadCalendar?message.id=14436867

The SAP community thrives on open discussions and shared learning. I hope you'll join us on July 29 as we explore one of the most important SAP platform announcements of 2026 and gain direct insight into what it means for BTP and Business Data Cloud customers moving forward. We look forward to seeing you there.

Kind regards

Tania

Global User Groups Organization

entry.title: Re: Reverse 553 movement
entry.content:
Dear @N0050,

Movement type 553 posted through an inspection lot cannot be reversed in standard Public Cloud. Reset Usage Decision resets the usage decision data but does not reverse the stock posting. SAP documented workaround is to repost the quantity into quality inspection stock using the appropriate process and then process the newly generated inspection lot correctly. The original material document remains unchanged

If the scrapping was correct and only the cost center was incorrect the original 553 posting cannot be edited. The cost accounting line item can be corrected, provided it is eligible for reposting, using Repost Line Items Cost Accounting (F5549)

More details below:

  • SAP KBA 3359470 – Reset Usage Decision cannot reverse inspection-lot stock postings
  • SAP Help – Repost Line Items – Cost Accounting (F5549)

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise Webinar Series - two sessions this week on 14th and 16th July
entry.content:
We have Two sessions coming up this week in the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series, both directly relevant for anyone making architectural decisions around AI.
 
Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise is a 13-part webinar series designed by architects for architects. Each session unpacks a different dimension of the Autonomous Enterprise: from the Business AI Platform and agent development to data foundations, governance, and the Context System. It is a community learning journey where SAP architects, product leaders, and customers come together to work through the What, why, and How of building for an agentic world. All sessions are complimentary and available on-demand.
 
This week We have two sessions!
 
Session 7 - Monday 14 July | 17:00 CET / 11:00 ET / 08:00 PT An Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of Models With Markus Kohler, Simon Knehr , and Ashok Veilumuthu
SAP is the only company that has trained AI models on the full depth of its institutional knowledge — decades of business process expertise, application code, financial outcomes, and industry best practices. This session explores what the SAP Family of Models is, how business context delivers 3.5x higher prediction quality than generic LLMs, and what it means for architects evaluating AI model strategies. If you are deciding between generic foundation models and purpose-built enterprise models — or trying to explain the difference to your leadership — this is the session.
 
Session 8 - Wednesday 16 July | 14:00 CET / 08:00 ET / 05:00 PT -  The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to Reality
 
Every architect asks: what does the target architecture look like, and how do I get there? This session answers both - covering the AI-Native North Star Architecture (how AI, data, applications, and agents converge as a unified system), the AI Golden Path (a guided decision framework from idea to production) and the Architecture Center in General. Includes live demonstrations and worked examples showing how reference patterns translate into real implementation decisions. If you have been looking at the published assets on the SAP Architecture Center and wondering how to apply them, this is where it comes together.
Register once, attend any session: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

You can download and import the updated calendar file to ensure all remaining sessions are added to your calendar.

 Slides and recordings from previous session here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/slides-amp-recordings-from-the-13-part-architecting-the-autonomous/ba-p/14432238#M191674

Feel free to share this with to anyone in your architecture, AI, or technology leadership teams who would benefit.

However, there is a auto-response as below with helpful links and details. It could still be helpful for those who are not aware of Case creation process 🙂_______________________________________________________

Dear Partner,

Please note that TDD support has been updated to provide faster, more targeted assistance. As part of this change, this mailbox has been retired and is not being monitored.

For your support query, we kindly ask you to get assistance via the following channels:

  • Product and pricing information via SAP Partner Pricing app
  • Invoice and order requests (incl. termination, global account, entitlement queries) via Partnership Support in SAP for Me
    • Click on ‘Get Support’
    • Select ‘Test, Demo, and Development’
    • Select respective subcategory
  • Provisioning and technical requests via SAP for Me support

Learn more about the future support for Test, Demo, Development inquiries. For more information about TDD, please visit our SAP Partner Portal pages.

Best regards,

Your O2I Cloud & On-Prem Support Team

This mail is automatically generated. Please do not reply to this mail.

_______________________________________________________

entry.title: Re: SAC Dimension API Issue
entry.content:
@MK57 

You can configure the delimiter in the API url by setting $format=text/csv&delimiter=%7C

%7C is delimiter hex code for "|"

You can check the documentation below.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/14cac91febef464dbb1efce20e3f1613/db13b8e75e7443a7a3332d177c9b3bdd.html

 

Hope this helps!

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: how to create project billing request using API in sa...
entry.content:
Hello @yKim,

Good day!

If you’re on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, use the “Project Billing Request” OData API (service CE_PROJECTBILLINGREQUEST_0001).

Steps:
  1. Create a Communication Arrangement for the API (OAuth 2.0 or Basic auth) and note the service URL.

  2. Create the PBR header via POST to the header entity set (e.g., API_PROJECT_BILLING_REQUEST_SRV A_ProjectBillingRequest) with mandatory fields like CompanyCode, Project, BillingRequestDate, Customer, Currency, and a valid PBR type.

  3. Create items via the items navigation from the header (e.g., …/A_ProjectBillingRequest('<PBR_ID>')/to_ProjectBillingRequestItem).

  4. Submit the PBR by calling the bound “submit” action on the header entity (see Operations on the API page for the exact action name and URL).

  5. Verify with a GET (optionally using $expand to see items).
References:
  • Project Billing Request
  • Project Billing Request data product
Tip: If the submit step fails, check that your PBR type and status are configured correctly and that all mandatory fields (e.g., CompanyCode, Project/WBS, Customer, Currency) exist in your system. If you share the HTTP error and payload, we can help pinpoint the missing piece.

entry.title: Re: How to extract multi references from XML Bank Statements
entry.content: Hi Raymond, thanks for sharing the regex. As far as I understand in XML editor I always need to work with functions like regex-match or similar. Compared to text based processing for MT940 it does not allow direct input of regular expression. Or am I wrong? Therefore it is also not possible to have several rounds of regular expressions? Correct me if I'm wrong but this is the area I'm currently struggeling. It is not the reg ex itself, it is how to loop or extract several invoice numbers at once and have them listed comma separated so Bank Statement Processing can link the invoices from a collective payment

entry.title: Re: Best Practice / Legal Requirements for Retention of C...
entry.content: The system will keep all the forms and the data indefinitely until the forms are deleted or purged.  The legal aspects of how long these forms should be available is something you should ask you Employee Relations or legal professionals within your organization.

entry.title: User not as Member of Role after provisioning IAS to AS ABAP
entry.content:
Hi team,

 

we are currently testing user provisioning to AS ABAP following Docu: SAP Application Server ABAP | SAP Help Portal 

The connection succeeded and we were able to update user Attributes for a testuser.

We are testing the Role assignment with Group "ZS_SR_X_X_0257" wich is representative in CIS and our AS ABAP.

The testuser is assigned to this group in CIS, but wont get assigned in AS ABAP after a read or sync Job. 

The technical user has SAP_ALL so it wont be authorization issues. We can also see that the user provisioning is deleting all users wich were already assigned to that group in AS ABAP when we set parameter "ips.overwrite.existedbefore.assignments =true". So the groups is detected and can be edited.

 

Regarding to target system transformation (default from Docu) the groups are set as following:

{
                "sourcePath": "$.members[*].value",
                "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.USERLIST[?(@.USERNAME)]"
            }

In the provisioning log we can see that the system is updating the correct role "ZS_SR_X_X_0257" and user "XY"

"group:ZS_SR_X_X_0257,
system=SC2,
content={"ROLE_NAME":"ZS_SR_X_X_0257","USERLIST":[{"USERNAME":"XY"}]}"

Greetings
Rouven

entry.title: Re: SII Spain – Conflict between Operation Date/Fiscal Ye...
entry.content:
Hi @Chris1973;

First of all, thank you for your response and for the time you took to explain.

We looked into the date update job option, but ultimately it wasn't a viable solution for our case.

However, after further investigation, we found a configuration point that allows the dates to be aligned in a way that better fits our needs: Define Derivation Rules for Business Transaction Types (SSCUI 105884).

I wanted to share it with the community in case it's useful to others, and also thank you again for the initial guidance.

Best regards, and thanks again.

Álvaro Fernández

entry.title: Re: User not as Member of Role after provisioning IAS to AS ABAP
entry.content: Do you've all the Function Modules exposed in Cloud Connector? Are the group names identical to the role name? The technical user having SAP_ALL might not be enough, check SU53, because it sometimes goes over JAVA. https://me.sap.com/notes/3666721
